Chapter 17
Urinary tract disease
John Chitty
Urinary tract disease is common in guinea pigs. Much of  lying cause for lower urinary tract disease (see ‘Cystitis–
this disease affects the lower urinary tract, but renal dis- urolithiasis syndrome’, below). The urethra is distensible in 
ease also occurs and may well be underdiagnosed, as it  both sexes so, even though urolithiasis is common in the 
can be difficult to diagnose. guinea pig, urethral blockage is relatively uncommon.
Anatomy Renal disease
The guinea pig has paired kidneys and ureters leading to  Renal disease can be very difficult to diagnose definitively 
a single urinary bladder and then a urethra (Figure 17.1).  in guinea pigs, especially compared with cats and dogs. 
This means that the urinary system of the guinea pig has  Renal disease may be combined with, or secondary to, 
few distinctive features compared with other species,  other metabolic disease and may not always be recognized 
though the short urethra in sows is often cited as an under- in these cases.

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Causes
Many causes of renal disease are described in guinea pigs, 
although few have a clearly described pathogenesis 
(Fisher, 2006a; Reavill and Lennox, 2020).
■  Urolithiasis – uroliths can form in all parts of the 
urinary tract in guinea pigs and are frequently seen 
in the renal pelvis and ureters, as well as the 
bladder and urethra. They may cause obstruction 
and hydronephrosis. In some cases, the underlying 
cause – reduced urination and/or water intake 
– may also be associated with urinary stasis and 
infection ascending to the kidneys (Reavill and 
Lennox, 2020). Urolithiasis is discussed in more 
detail later in this chapter.
■  Infection – chronic nephritis, interstitial nephritis 
and pyelonephritis have all been recorded in guinea 
pigs. Coccidiosis and cytomegalovirus have been 
reported to be rare causes of nephritis (Fisher, 
2006a). Infection with Yersinia spp. has been 
associated with a case of pyelonephritis. Subacute  (a)
interstitial nephritis has been recorded in gnotobi-
otic guinea pigs seropositive for Encephalitozoon 
cuniculi (Boot et al., 1988). However, it is important 
to stress that clinical encephalitozoonosis has been 
recorded only in laboratory guinea pigs and not in 
pet animals. As such, encephalitozoonosis should 
be regarded as a very rare differential diagnosis in 
practice.
■  Metabolic disease – renal disease has been seen 
secondary to diabetes mellitus and pregnancy 
toxaemia.
■  Soft tissue calcification may be seen as a cause 
of renal disease. This may be linked to an inappro-
priate diet, especially when guinea pigs are fed 
(b)
diets intended for rats or rabbits. Low-magnesium 
or high-phosphorus diets may also be associated  Nephrosclerosis identified by post-mortem examination

seen in practice and manifests as pitting or rough- Signs of abdominal pain may be seen or elicited on palpa-

vated blood pressure (Percy and Barthold, 2001)  to obtain tissue for histopathological examination.

As cystitis often appears to be seen in conjunction with 
other chronic disease – for example, cystic ovarian disease, 
osteoarthritis, dental disease or middle ear disease – there 
may also be signs relating to these underlying or concur-
rent problems.
Diagnosis

tion of the bladder lining (white arrow) and nephrolith formation 
(red arrow). (b) A similar situation to that shown in (a) with calcification of 
the bladder lining (white arrow) and nephrolith formation (red arrow) 
evident. Note the gastrointestinal ileus (the reason for radiography); the 
uroliths were incidental findings. However, both the gastrointestinal and 
urinary signs in this animal probably resulted from an underlying inflamma-
tory focus – in this case, advanced middle ear disease (not shown on this 
radiograph). (c) Bladder uroliths (white arrow) and nephroliths (red arrows). 
(di) Lateral and (dii) dorsoventral radiographs of the same animal, showing 
urolith formation in the bladder (white arrows), ureters (arrowheads) and 
kidneys (red arrows). (e) Multiple small stones in the bladder and ureter. (f) 
A single urolith is visible in this case, and the bladder wall is markedly 
thickened. The author would normally manage cases similar to these 
medically, as the rate of recurrence of uroliths is very high. Surgery would 
(f)
be indicated in cases where obstruction occurred or where bladder pain 
could not be controlled.

Chapter 17 • Urinary tract disease
Prevention and treatment of idiopathic cystitis
■  Behaviour/husbandry – affected animals need to be encouraged to move and drink/urinate more 
frequently. Wild cavies live in dense vegetation; for domestic guinea pigs, the provision of a deep-litter 
husbandry system with at least 10 cm depth of good-quality soft hay (old and poor-quality hay is more 
likely to cause hay poke eye injuries) will increase the animals’ activity levels as they will move around 
under the hay. Owners of guinea pigs that are kept alone should be encouraged to obtain companions for 
them, as guinea pigs naturally live in groups. Although introducing new animals will increase stress in the 
short term while they are initially mixing, guinea pigs will become more active if they have companions.
■  Drinking water – more water bowls, or drinkers if the guinea pig prefers them (if in doubt, both should be 
provided), should be placed throughout the housing and run. There should be at least one per animal plus 
one more, to ensure that no individual animal can dominate access to the water. In addition, green leaves 
can be washed and fed while still wet to increase the animals’ water intake.
■  Diet – plenty of fresh green fibrous leaves and vegetables should be given, as well as ad libitum hay (see 
Chapter 2). Concentrate pellets should be less heavily relied on as a feed. Some practitioners believe that 
hypovitaminosis C may be involved in the aetiology of cystitis and urolithiasis, as vitamin C is involved in 
membrane integrity. Lesions of hypovitaminosis C are often seen on radiography in these cases, though 
these may be coincidental. In any case, providing a vitamin C supplement in the feed and/or adding more 
foodstuffs that are high in vitamin C to the animal’s ration is unlikely to cause harm.
■  Dietary calcium – there is no need to restrict dietary calcium if the animal is consuming enough water. If 
the animal is not drinking adequately and cannot be persuaded to do so, then a moderate reduction in 
dietary calcium intake will improve the calcium–water balance (see Chapter 2).
■  Anxiolysis – for animals that show signs of stress, administration of a mild anxiolytic may assist in the 
early phases of treatment, to encourage them to hide less and move more. Anecdotally, the author uses a 
nutraceutical agent containing ʟ-tryptophan and gamma-aminobutyric acid that does not appear to cause 
sedation.
■  Anti-inflammatory agents – the primary lesion in cystitis is inflammation, so anti-inflammatory agents 
are the mainstay of therapy. Initially, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (e.g. meloxicam; see Appendix 
2) may be used along with glucosamine supplementation. In cases that are more severe or that do not 
respond to initial treatment, maropitant appears to be effective as an additional anti-inflammatory agent.
■  In cases that do not respond to anti-inflammatory treatments, pentosan polysulphate sodium adminis-
tered weekly for 4 weeks may be of benefit. In cases where the animal is showing signs of severe pain, 
treatment with an opiate (see Appendix 2) may be considered in the short term. Where there is no 
response to this treatment, the animal’s quality of life must be considered.

Clitoral fossa/urethral uroliths
In sows, uroliths can be found at the entrance to the ure-
thra. Some texts describe these as being located in a 
diverticulum in the urethra, whereas others describe them 
as forming in the clitoral fossa, which is immediately adja- (a)
cent to the urethral opening. It can be difficult to identify 
exactly which anatomical structure is affected because of 
the distortion caused by the stone.
These uroliths cause extreme pain. Any sow showing 
signs of severe pain and vocalizing on urination should be 
examined for uroliths in this region. They may be palpated 
easily in the cranial part of the anus, although they may 
be overlooked if palpation is not performed. Radiography 
is indicated to check whether there are other uroliths in 
other parts of the urinary system (Figure 17.13); if any are 
identified, they should be managed as described above.
For removal of stones at the opening of the urethra or 
in the clitoral fossa, the sow should be anaesthetized. It 
may be possible to manually express the urolith after 
muscular relaxation (Figure 17.14). If the urolith is too large 
to be removed via the urethral or clitoral fossa opening, a 
small incision may be made over the stone to facilitate 
removal. This incision is allowed to heal by secondary  (b)
intention (Figure 17.15). Alternatively, narrow forceps or

endoscopic forceps can be introduced via the urethral or  17.14
after removal.
clitoral fossa opening and used to crush the stone. (© John Chitty)
